description: Loose granular material formed from broken rock, found on beaches and in deserts. 'Sheru' names the countless tiny fragments—what remains when stone surrenders to time and water, the soft accumulation of ages.
sound_symbolism: The word shifts and settles. 'She' /ʃe/ whispers like grains sliding, countless particles in motion. Then 'ru' settles with rounded rest, the sand coming to temporary stillness.
grammatical_notes: Functions as a noun: 'lo sheru' (the sand). Takes the elemental classifier 'themo' when counted as masses or areas. Associated with beaches, deserts, hourglasses, and impermanence. Often uncountable in reference to its countless grains.

pillars.secular-buddhist-philosophy: Sand teaches impermanence through accumulation—each grain was once mountain, and time transforms all solidity to particles.
pillars.pre-industrial-wisdom: Sand marked time in hourglasses and provided material for glassmaking—the tiny grains serving human needs through transformation.
